summary: Gruber pays tribute to Roger Moore's James Bond legacy by highlighting 'The Spy Who Loved Me' as Moore's best Bond film. He anchors the piece around the iconic ski-cliff stunt, widely regarded as one of the greatest Bond scenes ever, and draws a characteristically Gruber connection between Bond's digital Seiko watch receiving text messages in 1977 and the Apple Watch doing the same today. He praises the film's complete package — great opening, iconic car, memorable villain, perfect theme song — and quotes Moore's own self-aware assessment of what distinguished his Bond from Connery's and Craig's.
key_points: The ski-cliff parachute stunt in 'The Spy Who Loved Me' is arguably the greatest Bond stunt of all time, performed for real by stuntman Rick Sylvester
Bond's 1977 Seiko watch receiving secure text messages was sheer fantasy then but is now a commonplace feature on smartwatches like the Apple Watch
Roger Moore's self-awareness about his Bond — a comedic lover rather than a killer — was key to what made his portrayal work
The film succeeds as a complete package: great opening, iconic Lotus Esprit, Jaws as villain, Carly Simon's theme song, and Moore at his peak
money_quotes: At the time, sheer fantasy; today, a feature many of you reading this now have on your own watch.
I don't know if it's the single best Bond scene of all time, but it's up there, and it's almost certainly the best Bond stunt of all time.
I just love how the fall takes place in silence.
Moore's Bond had fun doing his job.
I look like a comedic lover, and Sean in particular, and Daniel Craig now, they are killers. They look like killers.
key_insight: The best Bond moments combine genuine spectacle with character — and Moore's self-aware charm, like the film's blend of real stunt work and fantasy gadgetry, is what made his era distinctive.
